title: 9.9 Excretory System
expanded_description: An introduction to the excretory system—the organ system that removes metabolic wastes from the body and maintains fluid and electrolyte balance. This guide explains the structure of the kidneys and nephrons (the functional units), the three steps of urine formation (filtration, reabsorption, secretion), how the kidneys regulate water balance (with antidiuretic hormone), and common kidney disorders. It also covers the other excretory organs (skin, lungs, liver) and how they contribute to waste removal.

core_concept.principle: The kidneys filter blood to remove wastes and regulate water and electrolyte balance. Each kidney contains about 1 million nephrons—tiny filtering units. Urine formation involves three processes: filtration (blood plasma is filtered into the nephron), reabsorption (useful substances like water, glucose, and amino acids are returned to blood), and secretion (additional wastes are added to urine). The final urine is stored in the bladder and eliminated.
core_concept.explanation:
Your body produces wastes that must be removed. The most toxic waste is urea, produced when proteins are broken down. If urea builds up in blood, it can damage cells. The kidneys remove urea and other wastes, while also balancing water, salt, and pH.

The urinary system:
• Kidneys (2): Filter blood and produce urine.
• Ureters (2): Tubes that carry urine from kidneys to bladder.
• Urinary bladder: Stores urine until elimination.
• Urethra: Tube that carries urine out of the body.

Kidney anatomy:
Each kidney is about the size of a fist. On a cross-section:
• Cortex: Outer region, contains most nephrons.
• Medulla: Inner region, contains renal pyramids.
• Renal pelvis: Funnel that collects urine and drains to ureter.

The nephron (functional unit):
Each kidney has about 1 million nephrons. Each nephron has:
• Glomerulus: A ball of capillaries where filtration happens.
• Bowman's capsule: Cup-shaped structure that surrounds the glomerulus, collects filtrate.
• Proximal tubule: Where most reabsorption happens.
• Loop of Henle: Creates concentration gradient for water reabsorption.
• Distal tubule: Fine-tuning of electrolyte balance.
• Collecting duct: Final water reabsorption, carries urine to renal pelvis.

The three steps of urine formation:
1. Glomerular filtration: Blood pressure forces water, salts, glucose, amino acids, and urea out of the glomerulus into Bowman's capsule. Blood cells and large proteins stay in blood.
2. Tubular reabsorption: As filtrate flows through the tubules, useful substances (glucose, amino acids, most water, needed salts) are transported back into blood.
3. Tubular secretion: Additional wastes (hydrogen ions, potassium, drugs) are actively pumped from blood into the tubule to be eliminated in urine.

What ends up in urine:
• Water (excess only—most is reabsorbed)
• Urea (from protein breakdown)
• Uric acid (from DNA/RNA breakdown)
• Creatinine (from muscle breakdown)
• Excess ions (sodium, potassium, chloride)
• Hydrogen ions (for pH balance)
• Drugs and toxins

Fun fact: Your kidneys filter your entire blood volume about 60 times per day! That's about 180 liters (about 50 gallons) of filtrate produced daily. But you only excrete about 1-2 liters of urine—the rest (99%) is reabsorbed. This shows how efficient the kidneys are at reclaiming useful substances.

image.alt: A diagram of the urinary system: kidneys (bean-shaped), ureters (tubes to bladder), bladder (round), urethra. A cross-section of the kidney showing cortex, medulla, renal pyramids, renal pelvis, and ureter. A close-up of a nephron showing glomerulus (ball of capillaries), Bowman's capsule, proximal tubule, loop of Henle, distal tubule, and collecting duct.
image.caption: The urinary system and nephron: Kidneys filter blood to produce urine. Nephrons are the functional units where filtration, reabsorption, and secretion happen.

sections.section_title: Introduction: Why We Need to Excrete
sections.introduction:
Every day, your cells produce waste products. Just like your house needs trash removal, your body needs to get rid of wastes. The excretory system is your body's waste management service.

What wastes need to be removed?
• Urea: From protein breakdown. If urea builds up, it's toxic to cells.
• Carbon dioxide: From cellular respiration. Removed by lungs (exhalation).
• Water and salts: Excess must be removed to maintain balance.
• Uric acid: From DNA/RNA breakdown. Too much can cause gout.
• Creatinine: From muscle breakdown. Used as a marker of kidney function.
• Hydrogen ions (acid): To maintain blood pH.

Organs of excretion:
• Kidneys: Main excretory organs. Remove urea, excess water, salts, and toxins. Produce urine.
• Skin: Sweat glands excrete water, salts, and a small amount of urea.
• Lungs: Excrete carbon dioxide and water vapor.
• Liver: Processes wastes (converts ammonia to urea) and excretes some into bile.

Why kidneys are so important:
If your kidneys fail, waste builds up in your blood (uremia). Without treatment (dialysis or transplant), this is fatal. Kidney disease is often silent—you can lose up to 90% of kidney function before feeling symptoms. That's why kidney function tests (creatinine, eGFR) are part of routine blood work.

Homeostasis: The kidneys don't just remove wastes—they also maintain balance (homeostasis):
• Water balance: Too little water → kidneys conserve water (concentrated urine). Too much water → kidneys excrete excess water (dilute urine).
• Salt balance: Kidneys adjust sodium, potassium, and chloride excretion.
• pH balance: Kidneys excrete hydrogen ions (acid) or bicarbonate (base) to maintain blood pH 7.4.
• Blood pressure: Kidneys release renin, which helps regulate blood pressure.
• Red blood cell production: Kidneys produce erythropoietin (EPO), which stimulates bone marrow to make red blood cells.

introduction_image.alt: A diagram showing the four excretory organs: kidneys (urine), skin (sweat), lungs (CO₂, water vapor), liver (bile, urea). Arrows showing wastes leaving the body.
introduction_image.caption: The excretory system includes kidneys, skin, lungs, and liver. Each removes different wastes from the body.

key_concept.definition: The kidneys filter blood through millions of nephrons. Urine formation involves filtration (blood plasma into nephron), reabsorption (useful substances back to blood), and secretion (additional wastes into urine). This process removes urea and excess water/salts while conserving what the body needs.
key_concept.context:
The kidneys are incredibly efficient. They process about 180 liters of blood plasma per day, but only excrete 1-2 liters of urine. The rest is reabsorbed—water, glucose, amino acids, and needed salts all go back to the blood. This is why urine is normally sterile and contains only what the body doesn't need.

What urine contains:
• Water (excess)
• Urea (main nitrogenous waste)
• Uric acid
• Creatinine
• Sodium, potassium, chloride (excess)
• Hydrogen ions (to balance pH)
• Trace amounts of other wastes

What urine normally does NOT contain:
• Glucose (indicates diabetes if present)
• Blood (indicates infection, stones, or damage)
• Protein (indicates kidney damage—proteinuria)
• Large amounts of ketones (can indicate diabetes or starvation)

Real-world connection: Urine tests (urinalysis) are a common, quick, and cheap way to check for kidney disease, diabetes, infections, and dehydration. The color, clarity, smell, and chemical composition give clues about health.

image.alt: A diagram showing the three steps of urine formation: (1) Filtration—blood plasma enters Bowman's capsule; (2) Reabsorption—glucose, water, amino acids return to blood; (3) Secretion—hydrogen ions, potassium, drugs enter tubule. Final urine composition shown at the end.
image.caption: Urine formation: Filtration, reabsorption, and secretion. The final urine contains wastes and excess substances.

sections.key_concept: The nephron is the functional unit of the kidney. It consists of a glomerulus (filtration) and a tubule (reabsorption and secretion). Different segments of the tubule have specialized functions—proximal tubule for bulk reabsorption, loop of Henle for water conservation, distal tubule and collecting duct for fine-tuning.

classifications.value: Understanding the anatomy of the nephron and what happens in each segment.
characteristics: Glomerulus: A tuft of capillaries inside Bowman's capsule. Blood pressure (about 55 mmHg) forces fluid out of capillaries into Bowman's space. Filtered substances: water, salts, glucose, amino acids, urea. Not filtered: blood cells, platelets, large proteins (albumin). The filtration barrier (fenestrated capillaries, basement membrane, podocytes) is highly selective.

Bowman's Capsule: Cup-shaped structure surrounding the glomerulus. Collects filtrate and directs it to the proximal tubule. Podocytes (specialized cells) wrap around capillaries and form filtration slits.

Proximal Tubule: First segment of the tubule. Reabsorbs 65% of filtered water, 100% of glucose and amino acids, 65% of sodium and chloride, and most bicarbonate. Secretes hydrogen ions and some drugs. Cells have brush border (microvilli) for increased surface area.

Loop of Henle: U-shaped loop that dips into the medulla. Descending limb: permeable to water, not to salt. Ascending limb: permeable to salt, not to water. Creates concentration gradient in the medulla (countercurrent multiplier). This gradient allows the kidney to produce concentrated urine.
Distal Tubule: Fine-tuning of electrolyte balance. Aldosterone (from adrenal gland) increases sodium reabsorption and potassium secretion. Parathyroid hormone increases calcium reabsorption.

Collecting Duct: Final segment. Runs through the medulla, collecting urine from multiple nephrons. Antidiuretic hormone (ADH, from pituitary) makes the collecting duct permeable to water, allowing water to be reabsorbed into the concentrated medulla. This produces concentrated urine. Without ADH, water is not reabsorbed, producing dilute urine.
classifications.behavior:
Different segments have different jobs:
• Proximal tubule: The workhorse. Reabsorbs most of the filtrate. It can't be selective about water—if it reabsorbs salt, water follows. This is called obligatory water reabsorption.
• Loop of Henle: Creates a salt gradient in the medulla. The descending limb loses water; the ascending limb pumps out salt. This makes the medulla salty (up to 4× saltier than blood), which draws water out of the collecting duct.
• Distal tubule and collecting duct: Fine-tuning. Hormones (aldosterone, ADH) adjust what gets reabsorbed based on the body's needs.

Why the loop of Henle matters: Animals that live in deserts have very long loops of Henle (more salt gradient, can produce very concentrated urine). Animals that live in water have short loops (dilute urine). Humans are in between—we can concentrate urine up to about 4× blood concentration.

Clinical connection: Diuretics (water pills) work by affecting different parts of the nephron. Loop diuretics (furosemide) block salt reabsorption in the ascending limb, preventing water reabsorption and causing lots of dilute urine. Thiazide diuretics work in the distal tubule. Understanding where they act explains how they work and their side effects.

examples.process: Loop of Henle: The Countercurrent Multiplier
examples.explanation:
The loop of Henle creates a concentration gradient in the kidney medulla through a process called countercurrent multiplication: (1) Descending limb: Permeable to water, not to salt. As filtrate descends, water leaves (into salty medulla), making the filtrate more concentrated. (2) Bottom of loop: Filtrate is very concentrated (about 4× blood). (3) Ascending limb: Permeable to salt, not to water. Salt is pumped out into the medulla, making the medulla even saltier. The filtrate becomes dilute. (4) Result: The medulla becomes progressively saltier from cortex to inner medulla (concentration gradient). (5) Collecting duct: When ADH is present, water leaves the collecting duct into the salty medulla, producing concentrated urine.

Why it's called countercurrent: The fluid flows in opposite directions in the descending and ascending limbs. This allows the multiplier effect—a small amount of salt pumping creates a large concentration gradient.

Analogy: It's like a fish ladder in reverse—instead of fish going up, salt is pumped up, creating a "salty pool" at the bottom that pulls water out of the collecting duct.

image.alt: A diagram of the loop of Henle showing: descending limb (water leaving, salt staying), ascending limb (salt being pumped out). The medulla gets saltier toward the bottom. A graph showing concentration gradient (300 mOsm in cortex to 1200 mOsm in inner medulla).
image.caption: The loop of Henle creates a salt concentration gradient in the medulla, allowing the kidney to produce concentrated urine when needed.

classifications.value: Understanding how blood is filtered, what determines filtration rate, and what happens when the filter is damaged.
characteristics: Filtration barrier: Three layers—(1) Fenestrated capillary endothelium (holes), (2) Basement membrane (negatively charged, repels proteins), (3) Podocyte filtration slits (foot processes). This barrier allows small molecules (water, salts, glucose, urea) through but blocks blood cells and large proteins (albumin).

Glomerular Filtration Rate (GFR): The volume of filtrate produced per minute. Normal GFR is about 125 mL/min (180 L/day). GFR is determined by: (1) Blood pressure in glomerulus, (2) Resistance in afferent (incoming) and efferent (outgoing) arterioles, (3) Permeability of filtration barrier.

Autoregulation: The kidneys maintain constant GFR despite changes in blood pressure (80-180 mmHg). Mechanisms: (1) Myogenic response—arterioles constrict when stretched; (2) Tubuloglomerular feedback—macula densa cells sense salt delivery and adjust arteriolar diameter.

What's filtered: Water, sodium, potassium, chloride, bicarbonate, glucose, amino acids, urea, creatinine, uric acid, calcium, phosphate. What's NOT filtered: Red blood cells, white blood cells, platelets, large proteins (albumin, globulins).
classifications.behavior:
Glomerular filtration is driven by blood pressure. The glomerulus is unique—it has high pressure (55 mmHg) because the efferent arteriole is narrower than the afferent arteriole. This pressure forces fluid out, unlike other capillaries where reabsorption dominates.

The filtration barrier is selective:
• Size: Molecules < 20,000 Daltons (like glucose, 180 Da) pass easily. Molecules > 70,000 Da (like albumin, 69,000 Da) are mostly blocked.
• Charge: The basement membrane is negatively charged, so it repels negatively charged molecules (like albumin). This helps keep protein in blood.

Clinical connection: Proteinuria (protein in urine) indicates damage to the filtration barrier. Causes: diabetes (diabetic nephropathy), hypertension, glomerulonephritis, preeclampsia. Small amounts of protein can be normal, but persistent proteinuria is a sign of kidney disease. Microalbuminuria (tiny amounts of albumin) is an early sign of diabetic kidney disease.

GFR measurement: GFR is the best measure of kidney function. It's estimated from blood creatinine levels (eGFR). A normal eGFR is ≥ 90 mL/min. Chronic kidney disease (CKD) stages:
• Stage 1: eGFR ≥ 90 (with kidney damage)
• Stage 2: eGFR 60-89
• Stage 3: eGFR 30-59
• Stage 4: eGFR 15-29
• Stage 5 (kidney failure): eGFR < 15

examples.process: Diabetic Nephropathy: When the Filter Fails
examples.explanation:
Diabetic nephropathy is the leading cause of kidney failure in developed countries: (1) Mechanism: High blood sugar damages the glomerular filtration barrier over years. The basement membrane thickens, and podocytes (filter cells) are lost. (2) Early sign: Microalbuminuria—tiny amounts of albumin in urine (30-300 mg/day). At this stage, damage can be slowed but not reversed. (3) Progression: As damage worsens, proteinuria increases (>300 mg/day), GFR starts to fall. (4) End stage: GFR < 15 mL/min, requiring dialysis or kidney transplant. (5) Prevention/management: Strict blood sugar control, blood pressure control (especially with ACE inhibitors or ARBs, which protect the kidneys), and lifestyle changes.

Why this matters: Diabetic nephropathy shows how systemic disease (diabetes) can damage the delicate filtration barrier, leading to progressive kidney failure. Early detection (microalbuminuria) allows intervention to slow progression.

image.alt: A diagram of the glomerulus: normal (healthy filtration barrier) vs. diabetic nephropathy (thickened basement membrane, podocyte loss). A urine dipstick showing protein. A graph showing GFR declining over years. A person with diabetes checking blood sugar.
image.caption: Diabetic nephropathy: High blood sugar damages the glomerular filtration barrier, causing protein leakage (proteinuria) and eventually kidney failure.

classifications.value: Understanding how the kidneys reclaim useful substances and add wastes to urine, and how hormones control these processes.
characteristics: Reabsorption: Movement of substances from tubule back into blood. Occurs by: (1) Passive diffusion (water, urea), (2) Facilitated diffusion (some glucose), (3) Active transport (sodium, glucose, amino acids). Energy for active transport comes from Na⁺/K⁺ ATPase on basolateral membrane.
Secretion: Movement of substances from blood into tubule. Removes substances that were not filtered (some drugs, toxins) or were reabsorbed but need to be excreted (excess potassium, hydrogen ions).

Key reabsorption sites: Proximal tubule (65% water, 100% glucose, 65% sodium), Loop of Henle (25% sodium, water depends on concentration), Distal tubule (5-10% sodium, calcium under PTH control), Collecting duct (remaining water under ADH control, sodium under aldosterone control).

Hormonal control: Antidiuretic hormone (ADH)—released from pituitary when blood is concentrated; increases water reabsorption in collecting duct (concentrated urine). Aldosterone—released from adrenal gland in response to low sodium or high potassium; increases sodium reabsorption and potassium secretion. Atrial natriuretic peptide (ANP)—released from heart when blood volume is high; increases sodium excretion (more dilute urine).
classifications.behavior:
Reabsorption is the kidney's way of saying, "I need that back." Glucose and amino acids are 100% reabsorbed—they should never be in urine. Sodium and water are partially reabsorbed based on need.

The kidneys have transport maximums (Tm) for substances like glucose. When blood glucose is normal (<180 mg/dL), all filtered glucose is reabsorbed. When blood glucose exceeds the Tm (about 180 mg/dL), glucose spills into urine (glycosuria). This is why diabetes causes glucose in urine—blood sugar is too high for the transporters to handle.

Hormones control the fine-tuning:
• ADH (vasopressin): When you're dehydrated, ADH increases. Collecting duct becomes permeable to water, water is reabsorbed, urine becomes concentrated (dark, small volume). When you're hydrated, ADH decreases, urine becomes dilute (clear, large volume). Alcohol inhibits ADH—that's why you urinate more after drinking (and get dehydrated).
• Aldosterone: When blood sodium is low or potassium is high, aldosterone increases. It tells the distal tubule and collecting duct to reabsorb more sodium (and water follows) and secrete more potassium. This raises blood pressure and corrects electrolyte imbalances.

Clinical connection: Diabetes insipidus is NOT diabetes mellitus (blood sugar). It's a disorder of ADH. In central diabetes insipidus, the pituitary doesn't produce enough ADH. In nephrogenic diabetes insipidus, the kidneys don't respond to ADH. Both cause excessive urination (polyuria) and thirst (polydipsia), with very dilute urine. Treatment: ADH replacement (central) or other medications (nephrogenic).

examples.process: Alcohol and ADH: Why You Pee More
examples.explanation:
Alcohol has a well-known effect on urine production: (1) Normal ADH function: ADH makes the collecting duct permeable to water. Water is reabsorbed, urine is concentrated. (2) Alcohol's effect: Alcohol inhibits ADH release from the pituitary. Without ADH, the collecting duct becomes impermeable to water. Water stays in the tubule and is excreted. (3) Result: Large volumes of dilute urine (up to 4× normal). This causes dehydration—which contributes to hangover symptoms (headache, dry mouth). (4) Other factors: Alcohol also directly inhibits sodium reabsorption, adding to fluid loss.

Why this matters: The alcohol-ADH connection is a clear example of how a substance can affect kidney function. It also explains why drinking water while consuming alcohol (alternating drinks) helps reduce hangover—it replaces the fluid lost from ADH inhibition.

image.alt: A diagram showing the pituitary releasing ADH (normal) vs. alcohol inhibiting ADH release. The collecting duct: with ADH (water reabsorbed, concentrated urine) vs. without ADH (water not reabsorbed, dilute urine). A person with a glass of water and a hangover.
image.caption: Alcohol inhibits ADH, preventing water reabsorption and causing large volumes of dilute urine—leading to dehydration and hangover.

principles.statement: The kidneys regulate water balance by adjusting urine concentration. When the body is dehydrated, ADH increases, making the collecting duct permeable to water, so water is reabsorbed and urine is concentrated. When the body is overhydrated, ADH decreases, water is not reabsorbed, and urine is dilute.
principles.application:
This explains why:
• Urine color varies—dark yellow = concentrated (dehydrated); pale/clear = dilute (hydrated).
• You pee more in cold weather (less sweating, body has extra water).
• You pee less when sweating heavily (body conserves water).
• Alcohol causes frequent urination (inhibits ADH).
• Diabetes insipidus causes excessive urination (ADH problem).

Everyday example: If you haven't drunk much water, your urine becomes dark yellow and smells strong—that's concentrated. If you've been drinking a lot, your urine is pale or clear—dilute. This is a simple way to check your hydration status.
principles.example: A marathon runner loses large amounts of water through sweat. The runner becomes dehydrated. Sensors in the brain detect increased blood concentration (osmolality). The pituitary releases ADH. ADH makes the collecting ducts permeable to water. Water is reabsorbed, urine output drops, and urine becomes very concentrated. The runner also feels thirsty, prompting them to drink. This system keeps the body's water balance within a narrow range despite large losses or gains.
principles.analogy: The ADH system is like a thermostat for water. When the house (body) is dry, the thermostat turns on the humidifier (ADH) to keep water in. When it's wet, the humidifier turns off, and water goes out.

image.alt: A diagram showing: dehydrated → pituitary releases ADH → collecting duct water-permeable → concentrated urine. Hydrated → no ADH → collecting duct not water-permeable → dilute urine. A color chart of urine (dark to pale).
image.caption: ADH controls urine concentration. Dehydrated = more ADH = concentrated urine. Hydrated = less ADH = dilute urine.

principles.statement: The kidneys regulate sodium, potassium, calcium, and other electrolytes by adjusting their reabsorption and secretion. Aldosterone increases sodium reabsorption and potassium secretion. Other hormones (ANP, PTH) also affect electrolyte balance.
principles.application:
This explains why:
• Low sodium (hyponatremia) can occur from drinking too much water without salt (overhydration).
• High potassium (hyperkalemia) is dangerous—it can cause heart arrhythmias.
• Some blood pressure medications (ACE inhibitors, ARBs) can increase potassium (by affecting aldosterone).
• Kidney failure causes electrolyte imbalances that can be life-threatening.

Everyday example: If you eat a salty meal, blood sodium rises. The body responds by increasing thirst (you drink water) and increasing ADH (you conserve water). The kidneys also excrete more sodium (if kidneys are healthy). Over time, the extra water dilutes the sodium back to normal. This is why you feel thirsty after eating salty food—your body needs water to dilute the sodium.
principles.example: In hyperkalemia (high potassium), potassium levels are too high. This can happen in kidney failure (can't excrete potassium), with certain medications (potassium-sparing diuretics, ACE inhibitors), or from massive cell breakdown (crush injury). Symptoms: weakness, tingling, and dangerous heart arrhythmias. The heart rhythm can progress to ventricular fibrillation and cardiac arrest. Treatment: calcium (stabilizes heart), insulin+glucose (drives potassium into cells), albuterol (shifts potassium), and potassium-binding resins or dialysis (removes potassium).
principles.analogy: The kidneys are like a water treatment plant that not only removes waste but also balances the mineral content. If the plant fails, the water (blood) can have too much or too little of critical minerals—both are dangerous.

image.alt: A diagram showing aldosterone: low sodium or high potassium → aldosterone released → sodium reabsorbed (water follows), potassium secreted. A graph of normal vs. hyperkalemia ECG (tall T waves). A list of potassium-rich foods.
image.caption: Aldosterone balances sodium and potassium. Hyperkalemia (high potassium) can cause dangerous heart arrhythmias.

principles.statement: The kidneys maintain blood pH (7.4) by excreting hydrogen ions (acid) and reabsorbing bicarbonate (base). This is a slower process than the lungs' pH control (minutes vs. hours/days), but it's more powerful over time.
principles.application:
This explains why:
• Kidney disease causes metabolic acidosis (blood too acidic).
• Chronic kidney disease patients often need bicarbonate supplements.
• The kidneys can compensate for lung problems (respiratory acidosis/alkalosis) over days.
• Urine pH varies from 4.5 to 8.0 depending on acid/base load.

Everyday example: After a heavy workout, your muscles produce lactic acid. The kidneys respond by increasing hydrogen ion excretion over the next day, restoring normal pH. Your urine becomes more acidic (lower pH) during this time.
principles.example:
In renal tubular acidosis (RTA), the kidneys can't excrete hydrogen ions properly or can't reabsorb bicarbonate. Blood becomes acidic (metabolic acidosis) despite normal lung function. Symptoms: fatigue, growth failure in children, bone disease (because the body pulls calcium from bones to buffer acid). Treatment: bicarbonate supplements (sodium bicarbonate, potassium citrate) to neutralize the acid.

Different types of RTA affect different parts of the nephron: distal RTA (collecting duct can't excrete H⁺), proximal RTA (can't reabsorb HCO₃⁻), and others.
principles.analogy: The lungs are like a fast-acting fire extinguisher for acid (blowing off CO₂). The kidneys are like the cleanup crew—they slowly remove the remaining acid and restore the building (bicarbonate) over hours to days.

image.alt: A diagram showing hydrogen ions (H⁺) being secreted into the tubule and bicarbonate (HCO₃⁻) being reabsorbed into blood. A pH scale (0-14) with blood at 7.4. A person with metabolic acidosis (fatigue, rapid breathing).
image.caption: The kidneys maintain blood pH by excreting hydrogen ions (acid) and reabsorbing bicarbonate (base). Kidney disease can cause metabolic acidosis.

sections.section_title: Key Components of the Excretory System

components.definition: Kidneys: cortex, medulla, renal pelvis. Nephron: glomerulus, Bowman's capsule, proximal tubule, loop of Henle, distal tubule, collecting duct. Ureters, bladder, urethra. Hormones: ADH, aldosterone, ANP, PTH, renin. Key processes: filtration, reabsorption, secretion, excretion.
components.equation:
Glomerular Filtration Rate (GFR) ≈ 125 mL/min (180 L/day). Urine output: 1-2 L/day (0.5-1 mL/min). Fraction of water reabsorbed: 99%.
Creatinine clearance (estimates GFR) = (urine creatinine × urine volume) / (plasma creatinine × time).
components.interpretation: Normal GFR is 90-120 mL/min. GFR below 60 for >3 months indicates chronic kidney disease. GFR below 15 is kidney failure requiring dialysis or transplant. Urine output < 400 mL/day is oliguria (too little); > 2500 mL/day is polyuria (too much).

image.alt: A summary diagram: blood in → filtration (180 L/day) → reabsorption (99%) → urine out (1-2 L/day). A table of normal GFR by age. A urine output chart.
image.caption: Key kidney parameters: GFR (125 mL/min), urine output (1-2 L/day), and reabsorption (99% of filtrate).

categories.description: Chronic kidney disease is the gradual loss of kidney function over months to years. It's often silent until late stages. The most common causes are diabetes and high blood pressure.
categories.detailed_mechanism:
CKD is classified by GFR and by the amount of protein in urine. Causes: (1) Diabetic nephropathy (high sugar damages glomeruli). (2) Hypertensive nephrosclerosis (high pressure damages kidney vessels). (3) Glomerulonephritis (inflammation of glomeruli). (4) Polycystic kidney disease (genetic, cysts replace normal tissue).

Progression: As nephrons are lost, the remaining nephrons work harder (hyperfiltration). This causes further damage—a vicious cycle. Eventually, enough nephrons are lost that the kidneys can no longer maintain homeostasis.

Complications: Anemia (low EPO), bone disease (vitamin D activation fails, high phosphorus), metabolic acidosis, fluid overload, high potassium (hyperkalemia), and uremia (waste buildup causing nausea, fatigue, confusion).

examples.explanation:
When kidneys fail (eGFR < 15), dialysis can perform some of the kidneys' functions: (1) Hemodialysis: Blood is pumped through a machine with a special filter (dialyzer). Waste products (urea, creatinine) diffuse out, and excess fluid is removed. Done 3×/week, 3-4 hours each session. Access: fistula (surgically connected artery and vein) or graft. (2) Peritoneal dialysis: A catheter in the abdomen fills with dialysis fluid. Waste products diffuse into the fluid from blood vessels in the peritoneum. Fluid is drained and replaced. Done daily (often overnight). (3) What dialysis does NOT replace: Hormone production (EPO, vitamin D activation)—patients need erythropoiesis-stimulating agents (for anemia) and vitamin D/calcium supplements. (4) Kidney transplant: Best long-term option for eligible patients. A transplanted kidney from a living or deceased donor can restore normal kidney function (but requires lifelong immunosuppression to prevent rejection).

Why this matters: Dialysis saves lives, but it's a treatment, not a cure. Understanding kidney function explains why dialysis is needed—and why kidney transplant is the best option for many.

image.alt: A diagram of hemodialysis: blood from patient → dialyzer (filter) → cleaned blood returns. A diagram of peritoneal dialysis: fluid in abdomen, waste diffuses into fluid, fluid drained. A person receiving dialysis. A kidney transplant diagram.
image.caption: Dialysis replaces some kidney functions (waste removal, fluid balance). Hemodialysis uses a machine; peritoneal dialysis uses the abdominal cavity.

categories.description: Kidney stones are hard deposits of minerals and salts that form inside the kidneys. They can cause severe pain when passing through the ureter.
categories.detailed_mechanism:
Types of stones: (1) Calcium oxalate (most common, 80%)—from high calcium or oxalate in urine. (2) Uric acid—from high uric acid (gout, dehydration). (3) Struvite—from urinary tract infections (can grow large). (4) Cystine—genetic disorder.

Risk factors: Dehydration (concentrated urine), high sodium intake (increases calcium in urine), high oxalate foods (spinach, rhubarb, nuts), high animal protein (increases uric acid and calcium), family history.

Symptoms: Severe flank pain (renal colic—waxes and wanes, radiates to groin), hematuria (blood in urine), nausea, vomiting. Stones <5 mm often pass spontaneously; larger stones may need intervention.

Treatment: Hydration (drink water), pain control. For larger stones: lithotripsy (shock waves break stone), ureteroscopy (scope to remove stone), or percutaneous nephrolithotomy (surgery for large stones).

examples.process: Preventing Kidney Stones: The Role of Hydration
examples.explanation:
The single most important factor in preventing kidney stones is drinking enough water: (1) Mechanism: When urine is concentrated, minerals (calcium, oxalate, uric acid) can crystallize and form stones. Dilute urine keeps minerals in solution. (2) Target: 2.5-3 liters of urine per day. For most people, this means drinking 3-4 liters of fluid daily. (3) What to drink: Water is best. Citrus juices (lemonade, orange juice) contain citrate, which inhibits stone formation. Avoid sugary drinks (increase risk). (4) Dietary changes: Limit sodium (increases calcium in urine), moderate animal protein, and for calcium oxalate stones, avoid high-oxalate foods (spinach, rhubarb) but don't restrict dietary calcium (restricting calcium can actually increase oxalate absorption and stone risk).

Why this matters: Kidney stones are painful and common (1 in 10 people will have one). Simple prevention—drinking enough water—is highly effective and costs nothing.

image.alt: A diagram of the kidney and ureter showing a stone. A CT scan image of a stone. A person drinking water. A list of high-oxalate foods (spinach, rhubarb, beets) and a note about drinking plenty of water.
image.caption: Kidney stones form when urine is too concentrated. Drinking plenty of water (2-3 L/day) is the most effective prevention.

sections.section_title: Worked Example: Excretory System Case Analysis

worked_examples.title: Case Study: Fatigue, Swelling, and Abnormal Labs
worked_examples.problem: A 60-year-old man with diabetes for 20 years presents with fatigue, swelling in his legs, and foamy urine. Blood tests: creatinine 2.5 mg/dL (normal 0.7-1.2), eGFR 28 mL/min. Urinalysis: 3+ protein, no blood. (1) What is the likely diagnosis? (2) What does foamy urine indicate? (3) What stage of kidney disease is this? (4) What are the risks of this condition?

step_by_step.calculation: Creatinine 2.5 mg/dL (elevated). eGFR 28 mL/min (normal >90). Urinalysis: 3+ protein (proteinuria). No blood (rules out some other causes). Patient has diabetes (risk factor). This is diabetic nephropathy (kidney damage from diabetes).

Foamy urine (like beer foam) is caused by protein in the urine (proteinuria). Protein changes the surface tension, causing foam. In diabetic nephropathy, the glomerular filtration barrier is damaged, allowing albumin to leak into urine.
CKD stages based on eGFR: Stage 1: ≥90 (with kidney damage), Stage 2: 60-89, Stage 3a: 45-59, Stage 3b: 30-44, Stage 4: 15-29, Stage 5: <15. eGFR 28 mL/min = Stage 4 (severe) chronic kidney disease.

Risks: Progression to kidney failure (eGFR <15), cardiovascular disease (diabetes + kidney disease = high risk), fluid overload (edema from protein loss), anemia (low EPO), bone disease, hyperkalemia. Management: Control blood sugar, control blood pressure (ACE inhibitors or ARBs protect kidneys), reduce protein in diet, monitor electrolytes, prepare for possible dialysis or transplant if function continues to decline.

worked_examples.final_answer: (1) Diagnosis: Diabetic nephropathy with chronic kidney disease. (2) Foamy urine indicates proteinuria (protein leaking into urine). (3) Stage 4 chronic kidney disease (eGFR 28 mL/min). (4) Risks: progression to kidney failure, cardiovascular disease, fluid overload, anemia, bone disease, hyperkalemia.
worked_examples.concept_applied: Understanding the glomerular filtration barrier explains why diabetes causes proteinuria. Understanding GFR and CKD staging helps classify severity and guide management.

sections.introduction: Urine formation happens in three steps: filtration, reabsorption, and secretion. Follow the path of a water molecule and a urea molecule from blood to urine.

process_flow.description: Blood pressure forces water, salts, glucose, urea, and other small molecules from the glomerulus into Bowman's capsule.
process_flow.mechanism: The glomerulus has high pressure (55 mmHg). The filtration barrier allows small molecules through but blocks blood cells and large proteins. About 20% of plasma is filtered (180 L/day).

image.alt: A diagram of the glomerulus showing blood in, water and small molecules filtering into Bowman's capsule, blood cells and proteins staying in capillary.
image.caption: Step 1: Filtration. Blood pressure forces water and small solutes into Bowman's capsule. Blood cells and large proteins remain in blood.

process_flow.description: Glucose, amino acids, 65% of water and sodium, and other useful substances are reabsorbed from tubule back into blood.
process_flow.mechanism: Active transport pumps sodium, glucose, and amino acids out of the tubule. Water follows by osmosis. Nearly 100% of glucose and amino acids are reabsorbed here.
process_flow.result: Filtrate loses water and gains concentration

image.alt: A diagram of the proximal tubule showing glucose, amino acids, sodium, and water being transported back into blood. Brush border (microvilli) shown.
image.caption: Step 2: Reabsorption (proximal tubule). Useful substances (glucose, amino acids, most water) return to blood.

process_flow.description: The loop of Henle creates a salt concentration gradient in the medulla, allowing water to be reabsorbed later.
process_flow.mechanism: Descending limb: water leaves (into salty medulla), salt stays. Ascending limb: salt is pumped out (into medulla), water stays. This makes the medulla progressively saltier.
process_flow.result: Medulla becomes concentrated; filtrate becomes dilute

image.alt: A diagram of the loop of Henle: descending limb (water leaving), ascending limb (salt being pumped out). The medulla is shown with increasing salt concentration toward the bottom.
image.caption: Step 3: Loop of Henle creates a salt gradient in the medulla, enabling water reabsorption later.

process_flow.process: Distal Tubule and Collecting Duct (Hormonal Control)
process_flow.description: Fine-tuning: aldosterone controls sodium/potassium balance; ADH controls water reabsorption.
process_flow.mechanism: Aldosterone: increases sodium reabsorption, potassium secretion. ADH: makes collecting duct permeable to water; water leaves into the salty medulla, producing concentrated urine. Without ADH, water stays, producing dilute urine.

image.alt: A diagram of the distal tubule and collecting duct. ADH shown making the collecting duct water-permeable; water leaving. Aldosterone shown increasing sodium reabsorption, potassium secretion.
image.caption: Step 4: Distal tubule and collecting duct. Hormones (ADH, aldosterone) fine-tune water and electrolyte balance.

process_flow.description: Hydrogen ions, potassium, and other wastes are secreted from blood into the tubule. Final urine drains to the bladder.
process_flow.mechanism: Active transport pumps hydrogen ions (for pH balance) and potassium (regulated by aldosterone) into the tubule. Some drugs and toxins are also secreted here.
process_flow.result: Urine collects in renal pelvis → ureter → bladder → urethra

image.alt: A diagram showing hydrogen ions and potassium being secreted into the tubule. The final urine flows from the collecting duct to the renal pelvis, ureter, bladder, and urethra.
image.caption: Step 5: Secretion and excretion. Additional wastes (hydrogen ions, potassium, drugs) are added. Urine exits via ureters, bladder, urethra.

sections.definition: These are more advanced concepts about kidney function—from the renin-angiotensin system that controls blood pressure to the adaptations of animals living in extreme environments.

process_types.energy_profile: The RAAS is a hormone system that regulates blood pressure and fluid balance. When blood pressure drops or sodium is low, the kidneys release renin, which starts a cascade leading to vasoconstriction and sodium/water retention.
process_types.direction: Low blood pressure or low sodium → juxtaglomerular cells in kidney release renin → renin converts angiotensinogen (from liver) to angiotensin I → ACE (in lungs) converts angiotensin I to angiotensin II. Angiotensin II causes: (1) Vasoconstriction (raises BP), (2) Aldosterone release (sodium/water retention), (3) ADH release (water retention), (4) Thirst. This raises blood pressure and restores sodium and water balance.
process_types.function: The RAAS maintains blood pressure and fluid balance. It's a target for many blood pressure medications (ACE inhibitors, ARBs, direct renin inhibitors).
goals: Understand how the kidneys control blood pressure
Learn why ACE inhibitors and ARBs lower blood pressure

primary_example.explanation:
ACE inhibitors (lisinopril, enalapril, ramipril) are common blood pressure medications: (1) Mechanism: ACE (angiotensin-converting enzyme) converts angiotensin I to angiotensin II. ACE inhibitors block this step, reducing angiotensin II levels. (2) Effects: Less angiotensin II → less vasoconstriction (lower BP), less aldosterone (less sodium/water retention), less ADH (less water retention). (3) Benefits beyond BP: ACE inhibitors protect the kidneys in diabetes (reduce proteinuria), protect the heart after heart attacks, and reduce heart failure hospitalizations. (4) Side effects: Dry cough (due to increased bradykinin), hyperkalemia (high potassium), angioedema (rare but serious swelling). (5) ARBs (losartan, valsartan): Block the angiotensin II receptor directly. They have similar benefits but don't cause cough.

Why this matters: The RAAS is a perfect example of how understanding kidney function led to major advances in treating hypertension, heart failure, and kidney disease.

image.alt: A diagram of the RAAS: renin → angiotensin I → ACE → angiotensin II → vasoconstriction, aldosterone, ADH, thirst. ACE inhibitors blocking the conversion. A blood pressure cuff and a pill bottle.
image.caption: ACE inhibitors block the RAAS, lowering blood pressure and protecting the kidneys and heart.

image.alt: A flowchart: low BP/low sodium → renin (kidney) → angiotensinogen (liver) → angiotensin I → ACE (lungs) → angiotensin II → effects: vasoconstriction, aldosterone, ADH, thirst.
image.caption: The renin-angiotensin-aldosterone system (RAAS) raises blood pressure when it drops. ACE inhibitors block this pathway.

process_types.energy_profile: Aquaporins are specialized proteins that form water channels in cell membranes, allowing rapid water movement. ADH regulates aquaporin-2 in the collecting duct, controlling water reabsorption.
process_types.direction: When ADH binds to collecting duct cells, it causes aquaporin-2 channels to move from inside the cell to the apical membrane. Water can now flow through these channels from the tubule into the cell, then out through aquaporin-3 into blood. Without ADH, aquaporin-2 is internalized, and water cannot cross.
process_types.function: Aquaporins allow rapid, selective water transport. They are essential for producing concentrated urine and for water balance overall.
goals: Learn how ADH controls water reabsorption
Appreciate the molecular basis of diabetes insipidus

primary_example.process: Nephrogenic Diabetes Insipidus: When Aquaporins Don't Work
primary_example.explanation:
Nephrogenic diabetes insipidus (NDI) is a condition where the kidneys don't respond to ADH: (1) Mechanism: Mutations in the vasopressin receptor (AVPR2, X-linked) or in aquaporin-2 (AQP2, rare) prevent ADH from inserting aquaporin-2 channels. (2) Result: The collecting duct remains impermeable to water, even when ADH is high. (3) Symptoms: Massive polyuria (excessive urination—up to 10-15 L/day), polydipsia (extreme thirst), risk of dehydration. (4) Treatment: Unlike central diabetes insipidus (pituitary doesn't make ADH), NDI doesn't respond to ADH replacement. Treatment includes thiazide diuretics (paradoxically reduce urine output), low-sodium diet, and adequate water intake. (5) Acquired NDI: Can be caused by lithium (bipolar disorder medication), high calcium, or low potassium.

Why this matters: NDI shows how a molecular defect in a water channel or its receptor can cause massive water loss—and why understanding aquaporins is key to treating water balance disorders.

image.alt: A diagram of the collecting duct cell: with ADH (aquaporin-2 channels in membrane, water flows through) vs. without ADH (aquaporin-2 inside cell, no water flow). A person with excessive thirst and urination. A mutation symbol.
image.caption: Nephrogenic diabetes insipidus: kidneys don't respond to ADH because of defects in ADH receptors or aquaporin-2 channels. This causes massive water loss.

image.alt: A diagram of aquaporin channels in the cell membrane. Water molecules passing through the channel (but not ions). ADH shown causing vesicles with aquaporin-2 to fuse with the apical membrane.
image.caption: Aquaporins are water channels. ADH controls aquaporin-2 insertion in collecting duct cells, regulating water reabsorption.

sections.relationship: The excretory system, centered on the kidneys, maintains the body's internal environment. Each kidney contains about 1 million nephrons that filter blood (180 L/day) and reabsorb 99% of it, leaving 1-2 L of urine. The glomerulus filters based on size and charge; the proximal tubule reabsorbs glucose, amino acids, and most water; the loop of Henle creates a concentration gradient; the distal tubule and collecting duct fine-tune electrolyte and water balance under hormonal control (ADH, aldosterone, ANP). This system removes nitrogenous wastes (urea, creatinine), balances water and electrolytes, maintains pH, and regulates blood pressure (via renin) and red blood cell production (via EPO). When kidneys fail—from diabetes, hypertension, or other causes—wastes accumulate (uremia), fluid overload occurs, and electrolyte imbalances develop. Dialysis can replace filtration, but kidney transplant offers the best long-term outcome. Understanding the excretory system is understanding how the body maintains homeostasis—the balance that keeps us alive.

summary:
The excretory system removes metabolic wastes and maintains fluid and electrolyte balance. The kidneys are the main excretory organs.

Kidney anatomy:
• Cortex (outer), medulla (inner), renal pelvis (collects urine).
• Each kidney has about 1 million nephrons—functional units.
• Nephrons: glomerulus (filtration), tubules (reabsorption, secretion).

Urine formation (3 steps):
1. Filtration: Blood pressure forces water, salts, glucose, urea out of glomerulus into Bowman's capsule. Blood cells and proteins stay in blood. GFR = 125 mL/min (180 L/day).
2. Reabsorption: Useful substances (glucose, amino acids, 99% of water) return to blood. Proximal tubule reabsorbs most. Loop of Henle creates concentration gradient. Collecting duct reabsorbs water under ADH control.
3. Secretion: Additional wastes (hydrogen ions, potassium, drugs) pumped from blood into tubule.

Hormonal control:
• ADH (antidiuretic hormone): Released when dehydrated. Makes collecting duct permeable to water → water reabsorbed → concentrated urine.
• Aldosterone: Released when sodium low or potassium high. Increases sodium reabsorption, potassium secretion → raises BP, balances electrolytes.
• Renin-angiotensin system (RAAS): Low BP → renin release → angiotensin II → vasoconstriction, aldosterone, ADH → raises BP.
• EPO (erythropoietin): Stimulates red blood cell production.

What's in urine:
• Water (excess), urea (from protein), uric acid (from DNA), creatinine (from muscle), excess ions, hydrogen ions.
• Normally NO: glucose (diabetes), blood (infection/injury), protein (kidney damage), ketones (diabetes/starvation).

Common disorders:
• Chronic kidney disease (CKD): Gradual loss of function from diabetes, hypertension. Staged by GFR. End-stage (GFR <15) requires dialysis or transplant.
• Diabetic nephropathy: High sugar damages glomerular filter → proteinuria → progressive kidney failure.
• Kidney stones: Crystals form from concentrated urine. Prevention: drink plenty of water (2-3 L/day).
• Diabetes insipidus: ADH deficiency (central) or kidney unresponsiveness (nephrogenic) → massive urination, thirst.

Dialysis and transplant:
• Hemodialysis: Blood filtered through machine, 3×/week.
• Peritoneal dialysis: Fluid cycled through abdomen, daily.
• Kidney transplant: Best long-term option; requires immunosuppression.

Understanding the excretory system—from the glomerulus to the collecting duct, from ADH to aldosterone—is essential for understanding how the body maintains water balance, removes wastes, and responds to changes in hydration, blood pressure, and diet.
